nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons, harbors, and outer reef slopes, typically among corals and coral rubble at depths of 2–35 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton (FishBase trophic level 2.7). In aquariums, generally accepts a wide variety of prepared foods including frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, herbivore preparations with marine algae, and high-quality flake and pellet foods.

avoidWith: other aggressive damselfish in tight quarters

summary: A small, hardy damselfish from the Indo-Pacific (Andaman Sea to Loyalty Islands), generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly though tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: Generally advised to be kept in aquariums of at least 30 US gallons (approximately 114 L); 120 L or larger is preferred to give the fish space to establish a territory and reduce aggression toward tankmates. Has been reported to tolerate a fairly wide range of conditions, which has made it popular with beginners. Aquarium-trade temperature guidance is around 22–26°C (72–78°F); FishBase wild-habitat data is somewhat warmer at 25.7–29.3°C, so the species likely tolerates the upper end of typical reef-tank temperatures without issue. Generally considered reef-safe and is not known to nip corals or invertebrates. Tends to become more territorial as individuals mature, particularly toward conspecifics and other damselfish — ample live rock with crevices and hiding spots tends to reduce conflict. A 2026 Wikipedia note describes the species as stress-sensitive and possibly prone to rapid health decline if disturbed, which is at odds with the beginner-friendly reputation in aquarium-trade sources; the aquarium-trade view is weighted more heavily here, but new keepers should acclimate carefully and avoid frequent rescaping. Juveniles in small groups are sometimes recommended for added color, but for long-term husbandry singletons or a bonded pair are generally advised. The common name 'Black Cap Damselfish' is also used in the trade. Coloration is typically dark brown to black on the upper head and back with blue streaks and a pale yellow or cream belly and tail.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. Demersal eggs adhere to the substrate; males have been documented guarding and aerating the eggs. Captive breeding in home aquariums is generally considered difficult to achieve consistently.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: size (3 in / ~7.6 cm), minimum tank 30 gal, semi-aggressive, reef safe, omnivore, water parameters 72–78°F / pH 8.1–8.4 / SG 1.020–1.025 / dKH 8–12. Notes that the species tolerates less-than-ideal water parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae), scientific name (Whitley 1961), max size 7.5 cm TL, native range (Andaman Sea to Loyalty Islands, north to Philippines, south to GBR and New Caledonia, recently Tonga), depth 2–35 m, wild temperature 25.7–29.3°C (warmer than aquarium guidance), zooplankton diet (trophic level 2.7), oviparous reproduction with male egg care, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirms 7.5 cm max size, central Indo-Pacific distribution, dark brown body with blue streaks and cream belly. Notes territorial defensive behavior and a stress-sensitivity caveat at odds with aquarium-trade beginner ratings; recorded in careNotes for context. Used only for taxonomy/description corroboration, not husbandry.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; demersal eggs guarded and aerated by the male. Captive breeding has been achieved occasionally but is generally considered difficult in home aquariums.
